options.text: Important - please read
Action needed: confirm attendance for Thursday security briefing
Meeting
Hi everyone, I wanted to ask you all something quick

rationale: An effective subject line is specific, front-loads the required action, and names the topic and timing so the recipient can triage and respond without opening the message. Stating the action, the event, and the day delivers all of that at a glance.

optionNotes.A: Vague urgency words convey no topic and no action; the reader still has to open the message to learn what is wanted, which defeats the purpose of a subject line.
optionNotes.C: A single generic word omits the action requested, the specific event, and the date, giving the reader nothing to act on or prioritize.
optionNotes.D: This reads like an opening sentence rather than a subject line; it is conversational, withholds the actual topic, and wastes the scannable space with filler.

prompt: You are writing a weekly project status update for leadership. Which opening structure best serves a busy senior reader?

options.text: Lead with the bottom line: "Status: on track. One risk (vendor delay) needs a decision by Friday. Details below."
Begin with a chronological narrative of everything the team did each day this week, then state the overall status at the end.
Open with an apology for the length of the update and a note that you will try to be brief this time.
Start with a list of every minor task completed, leaving the reader to infer whether the project is on track.

rationale: A status update for senior readers should be structured top-down: state the overall status and any decision needed first, then provide supporting detail. This lets the reader grasp the situation and the required action immediately, before deciding how deeply to read.

optionNotes.B: Burying the overall status at the end forces the reader through a day-by-day account before they learn whether anything is wrong, the opposite of a top-down structure.
optionNotes.C: Opening with an apology and meta-commentary about length adds no information and delays the substance the reader actually needs.
optionNotes.D: An undigested task list shifts the analytical work onto the reader, who must infer the status themselves rather than being told it directly.

prompt: Which sentence removes the ambiguity about who is responsible and by when, so the recipient cannot misread the assignment?

options.text: The report should probably be finalized at some point soon.
Someone needs to send the report over before too long.
Please finalize and send the report to me by 17:00 on Tuesday, 30 June.
It would be good if the report could be wrapped up reasonably quickly.

rationale: Removing ambiguity means naming the specific actor, the exact action, and a concrete deadline. Directing a named recipient to finalize and send the report by a stated date and time leaves no room for differing interpretations.

optionNotes.A: Hedges like "should probably," "at some point," and "soon" leave both the responsibility and the timing undefined.
optionNotes.B: "Someone" assigns the task to no one in particular, and "before too long" sets no measurable deadline.
optionNotes.D: A conditional wish with "reasonably quickly" neither identifies who must act nor fixes a verifiable due date.

prompt: A counterpart has not replied to your earlier request after one week. Which follow-up message is most appropriately polite and professional?

options.text: I'm following up on my request from last Monday. Would you be able to let me know the status by end of week? Happy to provide anything that would help.
This is the second time I've had to write. Please respond immediately.
I assume you've been ignoring my email, so I'll ask one more time.
Did you get my email??? I really need an answer right now.

rationale: A polite follow-up references the original message neutrally, gives the recipient the benefit of the doubt, proposes a reasonable timeframe, and offers help. This preserves the working relationship while still prompting a response.

optionNotes.B: Foregrounding the writer's inconvenience and demanding an immediate reply is accusatory and pressuring rather than courteous.
optionNotes.C: Assuming the recipient deliberately ignored the message imputes bad faith and is likely to provoke defensiveness rather than cooperation.
optionNotes.D: Multiple question marks and "right now" read as impatient and informal, undermining the professional register a follow-up should maintain.

prompt: You sent a partner agency an outdated figure in yesterday's briefing. Which message best acknowledges the error professionally?

options.text: Correction to yesterday's briefing: the staffing figure should read 38, not 43. I've attached the corrected version and apologize for the confusion.
There may have been a small issue with one of the numbers, but it probably doesn't matter much.
The figure was wrong because the database export I was given was out of date, so this really wasn't my fault.
I am profoundly and deeply sorry for this catastrophic and unforgivable mistake, which I will never live down.

rationale: Professionally acknowledging an error means naming the specific mistake, stating the correct information, providing the fix, and offering a brief, proportionate apology. This restores accuracy and trust without drama or evasion.

optionNotes.B: Minimizing the error as "a small issue" that "probably doesn't matter" fails to state the correct figure and leaves the recipient unsure what to rely on.
optionNotes.C: Leading with blame on the data source reads as deflecting responsibility rather than owning and fixing the error.
optionNotes.D: An overwrought, self-flagellating apology is disproportionate, draws attention to the writer's feelings, and still omits the corrected information.

prompt: You are emailing a senior official at a foreign ministry whom you have not met, in a culture that values formality and indirectness. Which opening strikes the most appropriate cross-cultural courtesy?

options.text: Hey there! Hope you're having an awesome week. Quick favor to ask.
Dear Director-General, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to respectfully request your guidance on a matter of mutual interest.
To whom it may concern, I need the following items handled as soon as possible.
Hi! Just cutting to the chase here since I know you're busy — here's what I need from you.

rationale: Cross-cultural courtesy toward a senior official in a formal, indirect culture calls for a respectful title, a warm but measured greeting, and an indirect, deferential framing of the request. This signals respect for hierarchy and relationship before substance.

optionNotes.A: Casual greetings and slang are too familiar for a first contact with a senior official and disregard the formality the culture expects.
optionNotes.C: Using an impersonal "to whom it may concern" for a named senior official, then issuing demands, reads as brusque and inattentive to rank.
optionNotes.D: "Cutting to the chase" with an immediate list of demands is precisely the directness that an indirect, hierarchy-conscious culture finds discourteous in a first approach.

prompt: You must brief a non-technical ambassador on why email was down for two hours. Which explanation matches an audience-appropriate level of detail?

options.text: The mail server's TLS certificate expired, so the MTA rejected SMTP handshakes on port 587 until we reissued the cert and reloaded Postfix.

Email was unavailable for about two hours this morning because of an expired security credential. It has been renewed, service is fully restored, and we are adding an automatic renewal reminder to prevent a recurrence.
There was a problem and now it's fixed.
We had an issue with the cert chain and the OCSP stapling, plus some DNS caching weirdness, but I won't bore you with the packet captures.

rationale: Matching detail to a non-technical executive means explaining the impact, the cause in plain terms, the current status, and the preventive step, without jargon the reader cannot use. This gives the ambassador exactly what they need to understand and trust the outcome.

optionNotes.A: Protocol names, ports, and software internals exceed what a non-technical reader can interpret and obscure the impact and resolution they actually care about.
optionNotes.C: Stripping out the cause, the duration, and the preventive measure leaves the executive with no real understanding or assurance.
optionNotes.D: Listing technical terms only to say you won't explain them still burdens the reader with jargon while withholding the clear summary they need.

prompt: At the end of a message, you need a colleague to approve a budget line. Which closing provides the clearest call to action?

options.text: Let me know your thoughts whenever.
Please reply with your approval of the €4,000 line item by Thursday 26 June so I can submit the purchase order.
Hopefully we can move this forward at some point.
Anyway, that's where things stand for now.

rationale: A clear call to action specifies exactly what the reader must do, the precise item involved, the deadline, and why it matters. Requesting a named approval by a stated date so a purchase order can proceed leaves no doubt about the next step.

optionNotes.A: "Your thoughts whenever" requests no specific decision and sets no deadline, so the reader has no defined action to take.
optionNotes.C: A vague hope to "move this forward at some point" names neither the action required nor a date, so nothing will reliably happen.
optionNotes.D: Merely summarizing the current state gives the reader no request at all, leaving the needed approval unprompted.

prompt: Which sentence in an internal policy notice uses the most neutral and inclusive language?

options.text: Each employee should submit his timesheet to the man manning the front desk before he leaves.
Every staff member should submit their timesheet to the person staffing the front desk before leaving.
Every guy on the team needs to hand his timesheet to the front-desk girl before heading out.
Each man should give his timesheet to whoever mans the desk before he goes.

rationale: Neutral, inclusive wording avoids assuming a person's gender, using singular "they" for an unspecified individual and gender-neutral role terms such as "staff member" and "the person staffing the desk." This includes everyone without singling out a gender.

optionNotes.A: Defaulting to "his" for every employee and "the man manning" the desk presumes the people involved are male and excludes others.
optionNotes.C: "Every guy" and "the front-desk girl" are informal and gendered, treating roles as inherently male or female.
optionNotes.D: "Each man," "his," and "mans the desk" repeatedly default to masculine terms for people whose gender is unspecified.
